schools.summary: Gill: the servants here are those 'under the yoke' — not the yoke of the law of God or of Christ (though they were under both) but 'under the yoke of government,' in a state of servitude, under the rule of men and in their service; these are to count their own masters worthy of honour; Paul charging that servants under the yoke count their masters worthy of all honour, lest God's name and doctrine be blasphemed — the honour servants owe their masters to guard the gospel's good name (Paul directing Christian slaves, very many of the early believers, in their hard lot: those 'under the yoke' of servitude to 'count their own masters worthy of all honour,' yielding due respect, reverence, and faithful service, and that with a weighty motive, 'that the name of God and his doctrine be not blasphemed,' lest, by insolent or unfaithful servants, the gospel be reproached as breeding rebellion, the believer's lowly faithfulness being made to adorn the doctrine).

themes: let as many servants as are under the yoke count their own masters worthy of all honour
that the name of God and his doctrine be not blasphemed
the honour servants owe their masters
lowly faithfulness adorning the gospel

schools.summary: Chrysostom: a teacher has need not only of authority but of gentleness, and not only gentleness but authority, and all these Paul teaches, at one time 'these things command and teach,' at another 'these things teach and exhort'; for if physicians entreat the sick, not for their own health's benefit but the sick man's, much more should the teacher; Paul charging that servants of believing masters not despise them because they are brethren, but serve them the more, since they are faithful and beloved — the servant's duty not to presume on Christian brotherhood (Paul guarding against a peculiar temptation: a servant whose master was also a Christian 'brother' might be tempted to 'despise' him, presuming on their spiritual equality to slacken his service; against this Paul charging that such 'rather do them service,' serve all the more diligently, precisely 'because they are faithful and beloved, partakers of the benefit,' fellow-sharers in grace, so the bond of brotherhood is a reason for better not worse service, and bidding Timothy 'these things teach and exhort').

themes: let them not despise them, because they are brethren
but rather do them service, because they are faithful and beloved
the servant's duty not to presume on brotherhood
brotherhood a reason for better service

schools.summary: Gill: to 'teach otherwise' (or another doctrine, as the Syriac renders) is to teach a doctrine different from what Paul had now taught concerning the duty of servants to their masters, as the false teachers did, who despised dominion or government (not only civil government, speaking evil of rulers and magistrates, but church government), and unsettled the order of church and household; Paul describing the man who teaches otherwise and consents not to the wholesome words of Christ and the doctrine of godliness — the test of teaching by the words of Christ and the doctrine of godliness (Paul setting the standard against which every teacher is judged: the false teacher one who 'teach[es] otherwise,' a doctrine contrary to the apostolic gospel, and 'consent[s] not to wholesome words,' does not agree with the 'sound, health-giving words, even the words of our Lord Jesus Christ,' Christ's own teaching, 'and to the doctrine which is according to godliness,' the teaching that produces and accords with holy living, so the double test of truth is conformity to Christ's words and a godward, godliness-producing tendency).

themes: if any man teach otherwise, and consent not to wholesome words
even the words of our Lord Jesus Christ
the doctrine which is according to godliness
the double test of teaching by Christ's words and godliness

schools.summary: Gill: he 'is proud,' swelled and puffed up with a vain conceit of himself and his own notions, treating with a haughty air the faithful ministers of the word; for the doctrines of grace are of a humbling nature, especially when spiritually understood, but notional knowledge, knowledge of natural things, puffs up; Paul declaring that such a man is proud, knowing nothing, but doting about questions and strifes of words, whence come envy, strife, railings, and evil surmisings — the pride and quarrelsome fruit of the false teacher's empty knowledge (Paul painting the false teacher: for all his pretence he 'is proud,' puffed up with conceit, yet 'knowing nothing,' truly ignorant of the saving truth, and 'doting about questions and strifes of words,' morbidly obsessed with idle controversies and verbal disputes, and the harvest wholly evil, 'envy, strife, railings, evil surmisings,' jealousy, quarrelling, abusive speech, and suspicious thoughts, so the empty, contentious teaching breeds nothing but discord and corruption).

themes: he is proud, knowing nothing, but doting about questions and strifes of words
whereof cometh envy, strife, railings, evil surmisings
the pride and quarrelsome fruit of false teaching
empty controversy breeding only discord

schools.summary: Gill: these are men corrupt in their principles and corrupters of the word of God, who dispute in a very froward and perverse way, rubbing and galling one another, and so provoke to wrath and anger and every evil work; Paul describing perverse disputings of men of corrupt minds, destitute of truth, supposing gain to be godliness, and bidding Timothy withdraw from such — the false teachers who use religion for profit, and the charge to shun them (Paul completing his portrait: these are 'men of corrupt minds, and destitute of the truth,' their understanding depraved and the truth gone from them, given to 'perverse disputings,' and worst of all 'supposing that gain is godliness,' treating religion as a means of worldly profit, valuing godliness only for what it can earn, and from 'such' Timothy to 'withdraw' himself, to keep clear of these corrupt, mercenary teachers).

themes: perverse disputings of men of corrupt minds, and destitute of the truth
supposing that gain is godliness
from such withdraw thyself
false teachers who make religion a means of profit

schools.summary: Gill: by 'godliness' is meant not any particular grace but all the graces of the Spirit of God — faith, hope, love, fear, etc. — the whole of internal religion as it shows itself in outward worship and in all acts of holiness of life and conversation, which the doctrine according to godliness teaches; Paul declaring that godliness with contentment is great gain — the true riches of a godly, contented heart (Paul, turning the false teachers' phrase, giving the truth: not that 'gain is godliness' but that 'godliness with contentment is great gain,' true religion joined to a heart contented with what God provides being itself the greatest of all riches, for while the mercenary seek gain by godliness and find only loss, the godly and contented soul possesses, in God and in a quiet, satisfied heart, a wealth that no money can buy and no loss can take away).

themes: but godliness with contentment
is great gain
the true riches of a godly, contented heart
wealth no money can buy

schools.summary: Gill: this is a reason both clearly showing that godliness is great gain (since those who have it brought nothing into the world but sin, yet are now in such happy circumstances) and that godly persons should be content with what they have of worldly things, seeing they brought nothing in and can carry nothing out; Paul reasoning that we brought nothing into this world and can carry nothing out — the transience of all earthly possession as the ground of contentment (Paul giving the reason why the heart should be content: a man 'brought nothing into this world' at his birth, coming naked and empty, and 'it is certain we can carry nothing out' at his death, leaving all behind and taking not a penny with him; since all earthly goods are but a passing loan held for a brief span between two empty-handed states, it is folly to set the heart on them and wisdom to be content with what God provides for the journey).

themes: for we brought nothing into this world
it is certain we can carry nothing out
the transience of possession the ground of contentment
earthly goods a passing loan

schools.summary: Gill: 'food' includes all things necessary to be eaten and drunk, of which there is a great variety (here expressed in the plural), and 'raiment' every necessary covering, as the word signifies, and includes a habitation, which is a cover and shelter from the inclemencies of the weather; having these, we should be content; Paul charging that, having food and raiment, we be therewith content — the simple sufficiency that should satisfy the believer (Paul drawing from the transience of all things the lesson of contentment with little: 'having food and raiment,' the plain necessaries of life, enough to eat and to wear and a roof for shelter, 'let us be therewith content,' not craving abundance or luxury but resting satisfied when our real needs are supplied, for the believer who looks beyond this passing world finds in simple sufficiency, with God, all that he requires).

themes: and having food and raiment
let us be therewith content
contentment with simple sufficiency
resting satisfied when real needs are supplied

schools.summary: Gill: not 'they that are rich,' for some rich men are good men and do much good with their riches and are as free from temptations and snares and foolish and hurtful lusts as others (as Abraham, Joseph of Arimathea, Gaius), but 'they that will be rich,' who labour after it, make haste for it; Paul warning that they that will be rich fall into temptation, a snare, and many foolish and hurtful lusts which drown men in destruction — the ruinous snares of the craving to be rich (Paul carefully marking the danger not in riches themselves but in the determined craving for them: 'they that will be rich,' those whose set purpose and passion is to grow wealthy, 'fall into temptation and a snare,' trapped by the devil, 'and into many foolish and hurtful lusts,' a host of senseless and harmful desires, which at last 'drown men in destruction and perdition,' sinking them as in deep water into ruin both temporal and eternal, so it is the love and pursuit of wealth, not its possession, that is so deadly).

debates.question: Where does the danger lie — in riches, or in the craving for them?

sides.position: Not in riches themselves (some rich men are godly and do much good) but in the determined craving to be rich, which traps men in ruinous lusts

themes: but they that will be rich fall into temptation and a snare
many foolish and hurtful lusts
which drown men in destruction and perdition
the peril in the craving, not the possession

schools.summary: Gill: it is not money itself — silver and gold, which are God's creatures and his gifts and may be used to and answer many good purposes — but the love of it, and not any love of it (for there may be a lawful love and desire after it so far as it is needful), but the inordinate love; Paul declaring that the love of money is the root of all evil, which some coveting have erred from the faith and pierced themselves with many sorrows — the inordinate love of money as the spring of evils and ruin (Paul naming the deep cause of those ruinous lusts: 'the love of money,' covetousness, the inordinate craving for wealth, not money itself which is God's good gift rightly used, 'is the root of all evil,' or of all kinds of evil, the spring from which countless sins flow, and showing its bitter fruit: 'while some coveted after' it 'they have erred from the faith,' been seduced from the truth into apostasy, 'and pierced themselves through with many sorrows,' wounding their own souls with self-inflicted griefs, so covetousness is both spiritually and inwardly destructive).

debates.question: Is money itself the root of all evil?

sides.position: No — not money itself (God's good creature and gift, rightly used), but the inordinate love of it, covetousness, is the root of all kinds of evil

themes: for the love of money is the root of all evil
which while some coveted after, they have erred from the faith
pierced themselves through with many sorrows
the inordinate love, not money itself

schools.summary: Gill: Timothy is called 'man of God' not only by creation as every man is, nor merely by special grace as everyone chosen, redeemed, and sanctified is, but by his peculiar office as an evangelist and minister of the word, being qualified for, devoted to, and employed in the service of God; Paul charging Timothy, as a man of God, to flee these things and follow after righteousness, godliness, faith, love, patience, and meekness — the double charge of the minister, to shun the love of money and pursue the graces (Paul, turning from the covetous, addressing Timothy by the honourable title 'O man of God,' a man belonging to and serving God, and laying on him a twofold charge: 'flee these things,' the love of money and all its attendant evils, running from them as from a plague, and, on the other hand, 'follow after,' earnestly pursue, the cluster of Christian graces, 'righteousness, godliness, faith, love, patience, meekness,' the upright, godward, believing, loving, steadfast, and gentle spirit that should mark the man of God).

themes: but thou, O man of God, flee these things
and follow after righteousness, godliness, faith, love, patience, meekness
the double charge of the minister
flee covetousness, pursue the graces

schools.summary: Gill: the apostle suggests to Timothy that he had other business to do than to mind the things of this world; his life was a state of warfare, he was a soldier and not to entangle himself with the things of this life, having many enemies to engage with — Satan and his principalities and powers, sin, and the lusts of the flesh; Paul charging Timothy to fight the good fight of faith and lay hold on eternal life, to which he is called and which he had professed before many witnesses — the call to the spiritual warfare and the laying hold of life eternal (Paul, in martial language, summoning Timothy to his true work: 'fight the good fight of faith,' wage the noble warfare of the Christian life and ministry, contending for and by the faith against all spiritual foes, and 'lay hold on eternal life,' grasp and hold fast the life everlasting as the soldier's prize and goal, 'whereunto thou art also called,' the very end of his calling, and to which he had 'professed a good profession before many witnesses,' made open confession perhaps at his baptism or ordination, a profession he must now make good).

themes: fight the good fight of faith, lay hold on eternal life
whereunto thou art also called
hast professed a good profession before many witnesses
the noble warfare and the soldier's prize

schools.summary: Chrysostom: again Paul calls God to witness, as he had done a little before, at once to increase his disciple's awe, to secure his safety, and to show that these were not human commandments, that receiving the commandment as from the Lord himself and ever bearing in mind the Witness before whom he heard it, Timothy may have it the more fearfully impressed upon him; Paul giving Timothy charge in the sight of God who quickens all things, and before Christ Jesus who witnessed a good confession before Pontius Pilate — the solemn charge enforced by God and the faithful witness Christ (Paul laying his closing charge with the utmost solemnity, before two great witnesses: 'God, who quickeneth all things,' the living God who gives and preserves all life and can raise Timothy if he die in the warfare, and 'Christ Jesus, who before Pontius Pilate witnessed a good confession,' who, on trial for his life, fearlessly confessed the truth and his kingship, so Timothy, charged before the life-giving God and set the example of the faithful Confessor, might be moved to bold and faithful endurance).

themes: I give thee charge in the sight of God, who quickeneth all things
before Christ Jesus, who before Pontius Pilate witnessed a good confession
the solemn charge before God and the confessing Christ
Christ the example of the faithful Confessor

schools.summary: Gill: 'this commandment' may mean either what Paul last enjoined (to fight the good fight of faith), or the whole of the orders he had given throughout the epistle relating to the doctrine and discipline of the house of God, or rather the work and office of preaching the gospel committed to him; Paul charging Timothy to keep this commandment without spot and unrebukeable until the appearing of the Lord Jesus Christ — the call to a blameless, faithful keeping of the trust to the very end (Paul pressing the charge home with its scope: Timothy to 'keep this commandment,' the whole trust of his ministry and the faith, 'without spot,' unstained by error or sin, and 'unrebukeable,' so that no just blame can be laid on him, and that not for a season only but 'until the appearing of our Lord Jesus Christ,' to the very end, with the great day of Christ's return ever in view as the term of his faithfulness and the time of his reckoning).

themes: that thou keep this commandment without spot, unrebukeable
until the appearing of our Lord Jesus Christ
the blameless keeping of the trust to the end
the great day ever in view

schools.summary: Gill: though the time of Christ's appearing is unknown, the thing itself is certain; God will bring it about and make it manifest in his own time, the time fixed and appointed by him, which is only known to him and which he keeps in his own power and has reserved in his own breast; Paul declaring that God in his times shall show Christ's appearing, being the blessed and only Potentate, the King of kings and Lord of lords — the majestic titles of the God who governs the times of Christ's coming (Paul, having named the appearing of Christ, breaking into adoration of the God who appoints its season: 'in his times he shall shew' it, in God's own fixed and appointed time, and heaping up the titles of his sovereign majesty, 'the blessed and only Potentate,' the supremely happy and sole Sovereign, 'the King of kings, and Lord of lords,' ruling over all earthly rulers and infinitely above them all, so the timing of the great day rests wholly in the hand of the all-governing, all-blessed God).

themes: which in his times he shall shew
the blessed and only Potentate
the King of kings, and Lord of lords
God governing the times of Christ's coming

schools.summary: Gill: angels are immortal, and so are the souls of men, and so will be the bodies of men after the resurrection; but none of these have immortality of themselves — they have it from God, who only has it of himself, originally, essentially, and inderivatively; Paul ascribing honour and power everlasting to the God who only has immortality, dwelling in unapproachable light, whom no man has seen or can see — the incomparable glory of the invisible, immortal God (Paul completing the great ascription with the highest things that can be said of God: he 'only hath immortality,' deathlessness underived and essential, his alone by nature while all other immortals have it as his gift, 'dwelling in the light which no man can approach unto,' in a blaze of glory no creature can come near, 'whom no man hath seen, nor can see,' invisible to mortal eye in his pure essence, and to this incomparable God ascribing 'honour and power everlasting,' sealing the doxology with 'Amen').

themes: who only hath immortality
dwelling in the light which no man can approach unto
whom no man hath seen, nor can see
to whom be honour and power everlasting

schools.summary: Gill: some are poor in this world and rich in another, others rich in this world and poor and miserable in the world to come, and some rich in both worlds; Paul charges them that are rich in this world (in the things of this world); Paul charging the rich not to be highminded nor to trust in uncertain riches but in the living God who gives all things richly to enjoy — the right use of wealth, humble trust in God not in riches (Paul, returning to the rich, those who already have wealth as distinct from the craving would-be rich of v.9, giving them their charge: 'that they be not highminded,' not proud and lifted up by their wealth, 'nor trust in uncertain riches,' not lean on so fickle and fleeting a thing, 'but in the living God,' the sure and abiding object of trust, 'who giveth us richly all things to enjoy,' for it is God not their riches who is the giver of every good thing and even of the power to enjoy it, so the rich are to hold their wealth with humility and to trust the Giver, not the gift).

themes: charge them that are rich in this world, that they be not highminded
nor trust in uncertain riches
but in the living God, who giveth us richly all things to enjoy
trusting the Giver, not the gift

schools.summary: Gill: rich men sometimes do much ill to themselves, to the hurt of their bodies by luxury, intemperance, and debauchery, and to the ruin of their souls, and to others by fraud, oppression, and violence, for it is in the power of their hands to do much evil, and also to do much good as they should, and much is required of them; Paul charging that the rich do good, be rich in good works, ready to distribute, and willing to communicate — the charge to turn wealth to generous good works (Paul telling the rich how to use their abundance well: 'that they do good,' employ their means in beneficence, 'that they be rich in good works,' let their true riches be deeds of charity not hoarded gold, 'ready to distribute,' free and prompt to give to the needy, 'willing to communicate,' glad to share their goods with others, so the wealthy are to become wealthy in a better currency, laying out their money in liberality and love).

themes: that they do good, that they be rich in good works
ready to distribute, willing to communicate
the charge to turn wealth to generous good works
true riches deeds of charity, not hoarded gold

schools.summary: Gill: by such good works they are laying up a treasure in heaven, which will be for themselves to enjoy to all eternity, whereas what they lay up here is for others, for their children or friends, and sometimes for strangers (they know not who, whether a wise man or a fool, yea even for enemies); Paul declaring that the rich, by good works, lay up in store a good foundation against the time to come, that they may lay hold on eternal life — the heavenly treasure secured by a generous, godly use of wealth (Paul giving the rich the great reward of such liberality: in distributing and doing good they are 'laying up in store for themselves a good foundation against the time to come,' securing, not by merit but by God's gracious appointment, a sure and lasting good laid up for the future in heaven, 'that they may lay hold on eternal life,' that their godly use of wealth might be bound up with the grasping of life everlasting, so money rightly used becomes a means of laying treasure in heaven).

themes: laying up in store for themselves a good foundation
against the time to come
that they may lay hold on eternal life
money rightly used laying treasure in heaven

schools.summary: Gill: 'that which is committed to thy trust' is the gospel, a rich treasure put into earthen vessels, and ought to be kept pure and uncorrupt, faithfully dispensed, and diligently preserved, that it may be continued genuine and sincere and not either adulterated and depraved or taken away by false teachers; Paul charging Timothy to keep the trust committed to him, avoiding profane and vain babblings and the oppositions of science falsely so called — the guarding of the deposit against empty and false knowledge (Paul, with a personal and earnest 'O Timothy,' laying the climactic charge: 'keep that which is committed to thy trust,' guard the precious deposit of the gospel entrusted to him, and to that end shun two things: 'profane and vain babblings,' godless, empty talk, and 'oppositions of science falsely so called,' the contradictions of a 'knowledge' that wrongly bears the name, the pretended higher wisdom of the false teachers, which is no true knowledge at all, so fidelity to the gospel requires both holding it fast and turning from all that counterfeits it).

themes: O Timothy, keep that which is committed to thy trust
avoiding profane and vain babblings
and oppositions of science falsely so called
guarding the deposit against counterfeit knowledge

schools.summary: Gill: 'some professing' pretended to be masters of the above 'science,' boasting and making a great show of it and valuing themselves upon it; Barnes: some who professed to be true Christians, attracted by false philosophy, were soon, as a consequence, led to deny the doctrines of Christianity; Paul observing that some, professing this false knowledge, have erred concerning the faith, and closing with the benediction of grace — the ruin of those who embraced false knowledge, and the parting blessing (Paul, with a final warning, pointing to the sad effect of the 'science falsely so called': 'some professing' it, claiming and parading this counterfeit wisdom, 'have erred concerning the faith,' wandered from the true gospel into error and even apostasy, a solemn proof of the danger Timothy was charged to avoid, and then, in the briefest of closes, ending the epistle with his accustomed benediction, 'Grace be with thee. Amen,' invoking God's free favour upon Timothy).

themes: which some professing have erred concerning the faith
Grace be with thee. Amen
the ruin of those who embraced false knowledge
the parting blessing of grace
